ci: Stories are shared in different ways.
lo: Imagining stories together; Ways stories are shared; Connecting with stories

s.d: Students create their own imaginative digital puppet shows by first designing unique story backgrounds and characters in Delightex, then importing these into Puppet Pals on iPad to record and narrate their own stories. This workflow empowers students to visually express their ideas and retell stories in creative, multimodal ways, directly connecting to the unit's focus on diverse storytelling methods.

Students take photos of their story characters (drawn, built with Lego, or puppets), then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the characters' mouths and record their voices telling a story. This brings student-created stories to life and encourages oral language development while exploring different ways stories are shared.

Students collaboratively build a digital class storybook using Book Creator on iPads. Each child contributes a page with their own drawing, voice recording, and simple text, showcasing the many ways stories can be imagined and shared with an audience.

Students document their story-creation process by capturing photos and short videos of their developmental play (using costumes, puppets, Lego, etc.) and uploading them to Seesaw. They can add voice reflections about their stories, allowing families to see and hear the diversity of storytelling in the classroom.

Students use PicCollage on iPads to visually sequence the beginning, middle, and end of a familiar or original story using photos of their play, drawings, or props. This supports understanding of story structure and provides a visual, tactile way to share stories with peers.

Using the Design Cycle, students collaboratively design and build a large story map on the floor (using paper, blocks, or felt) representing the setting and events of a class-created story. They then program Bee-Bots to 'travel' through the story in sequence, retelling the narrative as the Bee-Bot moves from scene to scene. This hands-on STEM project combines storytelling, sequencing, and basic coding in a playful, age-appropriate way.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Stories are shared in different ways' through imaginative play, daily storytelling, retelling familiar stories, and hands-on activities using props, costumes, puppets, and digital tools. The unit emphasizes student agency, creativity, and multimodal expression, including a Mystery Storyteller program, narrative writing, and an Indigenous storytelling excursion.

ci: Everyone has a role to play in the community.
lo: My communities; Contributing to the local community; Connections in my community

s.d: Create an interactive digital 'Community Role-Play World' using Delightex, where students design and drag-and-drop digital avatars representing different school and local community roles (e.g., principal, librarian, groundskeeper). Students then use the iPad camera to record themselves acting out these roles and upload their videos to Seesaw, creating a multimedia showcase of community contributions.

Students take photos of staff members or community helpers (with permission) or draw their own, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short voice messages about each person's role in the community, fostering understanding of diverse contributions.

Collaboratively build a digital class book in Book Creator titled 'Our Community Heroes', where each student adds a page with a drawing, photo, or audio recording about a different community member's job and why it is important.

Design a large floor map of the school community and have students program Bee-Bots to travel between different 'community roles' stations (e.g., classroom, office, library), narrating the journey and discussing the importance of each stop.

Guide students to create vibrant visual collages in PicCollage representing the different roles and connections in their school community, using photos, stickers, and drawings. These can be printed or shared digitally to celebrate community diversity.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students use Sphero Indi cars and colored tiles to design and test a 'Community Helpers Pathway'?a collaborative, hands-on challenge where they build routes representing how different roles work together to solve a school problem (e.g., getting a lost item back to the office), iterating their designs to improve efficiency and teamwork.

plannerText: Prep students explore the central idea 'Everyone has a role to play in the community' by investigating roles within their school, creating wonderings, interviewing staff, and sharing their learning through videos and presentations. Activities include guided walks, role-play, collaborative projects, and reflecting on how individuals contribute to the community.

ci: Scientists follow a method to discover new things.
lo: The scientific process; Observing my world; Drawing conclusions

s.d: Transform the Prep classroom into a virtual science lab by using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes to let students 'hold' and explore 3D models of scientific tools, plants, and weather phenomena. This immersive experience allows young learners to observe, manipulate, and discuss objects as real scientists do, directly linking to the unit's focus on observation and the scientific process.

Empower students to document their Nature Detective Walk and Weather Watchers activities by capturing photos and short videos with the iPad Camera, then uploading them to Seesaw. Students can annotate their observations with voice recordings or drawings, making their scientific thinking visible and accessible to families.

After the Ice Melting Challenge or Rolling Ramp Investigation, students use ChatterPix Kids to animate photos of their experiment materials or 'scientist characters,' recording their own voices to explain predictions, results, and conclusions in a playful, memorable way.

Guide students to create a collaborative digital science journal in Book Creator, combining their drawings, photos, and voice recordings from each weekly experiment (nature walk, weather chart, ice melting, ramps, mystery materials). This living document showcases their journey through the scientific method.

Have students use ScratchJr to visually sequence the steps of a science experiment (e.g., the process of melting ice or rolling objects down ramps). They can animate simple characters to model 'predict, test, observe, record,' reinforcing the scientific process through coding and storytelling.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Challenge students to design and build a 'Science Lab Map' on the floor, then use Bee-Bots to navigate between different experiment stations (nature, weather, materials). Students plan, test, and iterate their Bee-Bot routes, reflecting on how scientists organize and follow steps in their investigations.

plannerText: Prep students explore the scientific method through hands-on experiences such as nature detective walks, weather observations, ice melting challenges, ramp investigations, and mystery material explorations. The unit emphasizes observing, recording, predicting, testing, and drawing conclusions, fostering inquiry and scientific thinking in authentic, age-appropriate contexts.

ci: Living things share our world and have an important role in nature
lo: The different living things around us; The roles living things play in nature; How humans and other living things share space
plannerText: Not yet documented in PDF.

ci: Relationships impact our wellbeing.
lo: Developing relationships; How those around us impact our behaviours and interactions; Keeping ourselves healthy and safe

s.d: Create an interactive digital 'Feelings Map' using Delightex where students drag and drop icons representing different emotions onto pictures of themselves and their friends. Then, app smash with Seesaw: students use the iPad camera to capture moments of positive relationship-building (e.g., sharing, helping), upload to Seesaw, and record voice reflections about how these moments impact their wellbeing.

Students take photos of themselves or a class mascot and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the image, giving it a voice to share 'What makes me a good friend' or 'How I feel when I help others.' These short, fun videos can be shared with the class to spark discussion about relationships and wellbeing.

Collaboratively build a digital class book in Book Creator titled 'Our Friendship Stories.' Each student adds a page with a drawing or photo of themselves with a friend, accompanied by a short audio recording describing how their relationship makes them feel and why it is important for their wellbeing.

Use PicCollage on iPads for students to create vibrant visual collages showing 'What friendship looks like at school.' They can combine photos, stickers, and emojis to represent acts of kindness, teamwork, and support, then present their collages to the class as conversation starters.

Guide students to code simple animations in ScratchJr that show two characters navigating a playground scenario?such as sharing, resolving a conflict, or inviting someone to play?demonstrating how positive relationships impact wellbeing. Students can narrate their stories and share them with peers.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Challenge students to design and build a 'Friendship Maze' using classroom materials. Students program Bee-Bots to travel through the maze, stopping at stations representing acts of kindness or friendship (e.g., 'Say something kind,' 'Help a friend'). Students iterate on their maze design and reflect on how each stop represents ways relationships support wellbeing.

plannerText: The Prep 'Who We Are' unit explores how relationships impact wellbeing, focusing on developing friendships, understanding how those around us influence our behaviours, and recognising balance in our interactions. Key activities include read-alouds, oral discussions, drawing emotions, role-play, and creating an essential agreement to foster positive classroom relationships.

ci: Emotions can be expressed in different ways.
lo: Recognising our own and others' emotions; Responding to and interpreting our emotions; Different ways we express emotions

s.d: Students create digital 'emotion avatars' in Delightex, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate their avatars with their own voices expressing different feelings. This app smash allows students to visually design a character and then bring it to life, sharing how emotions can be expressed through both art and voice in a playful, creative way.

Students document and reflect on their own emotional expressions by uploading photos, drawings, or short videos showing different feelings. Teachers can prompt students to capture moments throughout the day when they feel happy, sad, excited, or calm, and add voice recordings to explain their emotions.

Students collaboratively build a digital class book titled 'Our Feelings', where each page features a student's drawing or photo of an emotion, paired with their recorded explanation or a short story about when they felt that way. This supports multimodal expression and builds empathy as students learn about each other's feelings.

Students use Puppet Pals to create short animated skits where characters experience and express different emotions in various scenarios (e.g., losing a toy, making a new friend). This helps students practice recognising and communicating emotions through storytelling and role-play.

Students create visual emotion collages using PicCollage, combining photos of their own facial expressions, emojis, and colourful backgrounds to represent a range of feelings. They can label each collage with the emotion and share with the class, sparking discussion about how we show what we feel.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students design and build a simple 'Emotion Maze' for Bee-Bots. Each section of the maze represents a different feeling (e.g., happy, sad, surprised) using colours, symbols, or drawings. Students program the Bee-Bot to travel to a specific emotion, explaining scenarios that might lead to each feeling as part of their design process.

plannerText: This unit explores how emotions can be expressed in different ways, with students engaging in activities such as reading picture books about feelings, creating artworks and masks to represent emotions, participating in role-play and music to communicate feelings, and reflecting on their own and others' emotional expressions through group discussions and visual projects.

ci: Services are established to help the needs of people in the community.
lo: Local services; Why we need local services in our community; How services meet personal and community needs

s.d: Students collaboratively design an interactive digital 'Community Services Adventure' using Delightex to storyboard scenarios where community helpers solve real-life problems. They then bring these stories to life by recording voiceovers and animating characters in Puppet Pals, creating a choose-your-own-adventure experience that classmates can explore and respond to, deepening their understanding of how services meet community needs.

Learners document a 'Day in the Life' of a chosen community helper by capturing photos or short videos using the iPad camera, then annotating with voice recordings and drawings in Seesaw. This creates a multimedia portfolio that showcases their understanding of the roles and importance of local services.

Students create a class digital book titled 'Our Community Heroes', where each page spotlights a different local service. They use Book Creator's drawing, audio, and photo features to visually represent how each service supports the community, making the learning accessible and engaging for Year 1.

Children take photos of community helper puppets or drawings, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the images and record short messages as if spoken by the helpers. This brings the roles of community workers to life and encourages empathy and communication skills.

Students go on a 'Community Services Scavenger Hunt' around the school or local area, taking photos with iPads of people and places that provide services. They then create vibrant collages in PicCollage, labeling each service and sharing their findings with the class.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to create a model town map on the floor, identifying key community services (e.g., hospital, fire station, school). They program Bee-Bots to navigate between these locations, simulating how services respond to community needs. Students iterate their map and Bee-Bot routes based on feedback, developing spatial reasoning, sequencing, and problem-solving skills.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 1 students explore how services are established to help meet the needs of people in the community. Through read-alouds, guest speakers, research, and presentations, students investigate local services, their roles, and how they support the community. Learning experiences include interviews, creative presentations, and writing appreciation letters to community helpers.

ci: Patterns help us to interpret the world around us.
lo: Patterns and cycles in our lives; Different patterns around us; How patterns can be created, interpreted and extended

s.d: Launch an interactive 'Pattern Detectives' AR scavenger hunt using Delightex: students use iPads to photograph patterns and cycles they find in the school environment (e.g., leaf veins, brickwork, classroom routines), then upload and annotate their discoveries in Delightex. This app-smash creates a collaborative, visual map of real-world patterns, sparking curiosity and hands-on exploration.

Students create talking pattern creatures by drawing or photographing patterns (e.g., butterfly wings, water cycle stages), then use ChatterPix Kids to animate them with their own voices explaining the pattern or cycle. This brings abstract concepts to life in a playful, memorable way.

Facilitate a digital pattern journal in Seesaw where students document daily or weekly observations of patterns and cycles (e.g., weather, classroom routines, plant growth), using photos, voice recordings, and drawings to reflect on how these patterns help us interpret the world.

Guide students to collaboratively build a digital class book in Book Creator, with each child contributing a page about a different pattern or cycle (e.g., life cycle of a butterfly, days of the week, repeating shapes in art). Pages can include photos, drawings, and audio explanations, making learning multimodal and accessible.

Students design simple animations in ScratchJr to model and extend repeating patterns or cycles (e.g., day-night, seasons, animal life cycles). They can program characters to move, change, or transform in a sequence, visually demonstrating their understanding of how patterns repeat and change.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Challenge students to design and build a 'Pattern Pathway' mat for Bee-Bots, using colored tiles or shapes to represent different cycles (e.g., butterfly life cycle, water cycle). Students plan, test, and refine their mat, then program Bee-Bots to travel through the sequence, reinforcing both computational thinking and pattern recognition.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in exploring patterns and cycles in the natural world and daily life, including life cycles, seasons, and the water cycle. Students engage in hands-on investigations, digital research, and creative activities to observe, document, and interpret patterns, building critical thinking and research skills while making connections across science, art, and literacy.

ci: Plants are a life sustaining resource.
lo: Products derived from plants; Plants all around us; The interconnectedness of plants

s.d: Launch a 'Plant Detective AR Adventure' where students use the iPad camera to capture real plants around the school, then import their photos into Delightex to create interactive, augmented reality scenes. Students can add digital labels, voice recordings, and animations to explain how plants sustain life, turning the school grounds into a living, student-created AR plant encyclopedia.

Students photograph different plants and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the plants, giving them a 'voice' to explain their role in sustaining life. This empowers students to creatively demonstrate their understanding of plant functions and importance in an engaging, age-appropriate way.

Facilitate a collaborative digital 'Plant Life Cycle Storybook' using Book Creator. Each student contributes a page with drawings, photos, and voice narration about a stage in a plant's life cycle, resulting in a multimedia class book that documents their inquiry journey.

Guide students to collect leaves, petals, or seeds, photograph them with the iPad, and create digital plant collages in PicCollage. They then upload their creations to Seesaw, adding voice reflections about how each part helps plants sustain life, fostering both creativity and scientific thinking.

Students design simple animations showing the journey of a seed growing into a plant, including the resources it needs (sun, water, soil). This visual coding activity helps solidify understanding of plant life cycles and the concept of plants as life-sustaining resources.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students design and build a large floor map representing a garden ecosystem. Using the Design Cycle, they plan, create, and test routes for Bee-Bots to 'pollinate' flowers and 'deliver' water to plants, simulating the interconnected roles of plants, pollinators, and resources in sustaining life. This hands-on project integrates robotics, spatial reasoning, and environmental science in a playful, collaborative way.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 1 students in exploring how plants are a life-sustaining resource. Through hands-on investigations, digital research, and creative projects, students observe plant life cycles, document changes, and communicate their understanding using visual and tactile digital tools. The learning is grounded in real-world observations and student agency, with strong links to science, literacy, and transdisciplinary inquiry.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Homes reflect our culture and environment.
lo: Different types of homes; Characteristics of a home; The importance of sustainable living spaces

s.d: Launch a 'Global Homes Virtual Gallery' by having students photograph their own homes or favourite spaces using the iPad Camera, then app smash with Delightex to create interactive, 360-degree virtual tours. Students can add voice notes in their home language, highlight cultural features, and compare with homes from around the world, fostering empathy and global awareness.

Invite students to draw or photograph different types of homes (e.g., yurts, igloos, apartments) and use ChatterPix Kids to animate the homes, giving them a 'voice' to share facts about their culture, environment, and sustainability features in a fun, accessible way.

Guide students to collaboratively build a digital class book titled 'Homes Around the World', where each child creates a page about a unique home type, adding photos, drawings, audio reflections, and simple labels, supporting both literacy and cultural understanding.

Facilitate a 'Home Design Reflection Journal' on Seesaw, where students document their learning journey by uploading photos of their mini home builds, voice-recording their design choices, and responding to prompts about how their home reflects their culture or environment.

Have students create vibrant visual collages comparing two types of homes (e.g., a houseboat and a cave home) using PicCollage, incorporating images, stickers, and simple text to highlight similarities and differences in structure, materials, and cultural significance.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Challenge students to design and build a mini model village representing diverse global homes using recycled materials. Then, program Bee-Bots to navigate between the different homes, explaining the journey and the unique features of each home, integrating spatial reasoning, sequencing, and cultural inquiry.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 1 students explore how homes reflect culture and environment by investigating a variety of global home types, building mini models, engaging in comparative discussions, and showcasing their learning through creative projects and a learning showcase. The unit emphasizes hands-on inquiry, cultural appreciation, and student agency through open-ended building and reflection tasks.

ci: The actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing.
lo: Safe environments; How our actions affect ourselves and others; Keeping our body and mind healthy

s.d: Students create a 'Healthy Habits Hero' by drawing themselves or a character making a safe or healthy choice (e.g., washing hands, wearing a helmet). Using ChatterPix Kids, they animate the character to give a spoken safety or wellbeing tip, then upload their video to Seesaw for a class 'Wellbeing Gallery'. This app-smash empowers student voice and makes health messages memorable and fun.

Students collaboratively build a digital 'My Healthy Body Book', adding photos, drawings, and voice recordings about ways to keep their bodies and minds safe and healthy. Each page can focus on a different aspect: nutrition, exercise, sleep, or emotional wellbeing, allowing for highly visual, multimodal expression.

Students use Delightex to create interactive storyboards showing a sequence of actions that lead to safe or unsafe outcomes (e.g., crossing the road safely vs. unsafely). They can drag and drop images, sequence events, and narrate their thinking, building digital storytelling skills while reinforcing safety concepts.

Learners go on a 'Safety Scavenger Hunt' around the school, using the iPad camera to capture examples of safe and unsafe practices (e.g., tidy vs. messy walkways, correct vs. incorrect handwashing). They assemble their photos into a PicCollage poster, labeling each with emojis or simple icons to reinforce visual learning.

Students design simple animations in ScratchJr where characters make choices that affect their wellbeing (e.g., choosing to eat fruit, wear sunscreen, or take a break when tired). They can program cause-and-effect sequences, deepening their understanding of how actions influence outcomes.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students design and build a 'Safe Journey Map' on a large mat representing different areas of the school (e.g., classroom, playground, crossing). Using the Design Cycle, they plan, test, and iterate routes for Bee-Bots to travel safely between zones, adding visual cues for hazards and safe choices. This hands-on project builds spatial reasoning, teamwork, and reinforces the central idea that our actions influence safety and wellbeing.

plannerText: This unit explores how the actions we take influence our safety and wellbeing, with students engaging in activities such as daily read-alouds about the human body, building life-size body models, participating in healthy habits inquiry stations, and creating informative books about body parts and healthy choices. The learning is highly hands-on and visual, connecting to real-life experiences like the swimming program and culminating in student-led presentations and a Healthy Bodies Museum.

ci: Artistic appreciation and expressions inspire our imagination and creativity.
lo: Forms of expression; Expressing thoughts and ideas; Understanding and appreciating art

s.d: Launch an immersive 'Art Gallery of the Imagination' using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes. Students create their own digital artworks or 3D sculptures, then curate and 'hang' them in a virtual gallery. Peers can explore each other's creations in VR, leaving voice comments about what inspires them, making artistic appreciation a truly interactive, multi-sensory experience.

Students photograph their own artworks or creative projects, then use ChatterPix Kids to animate their creations and give them a 'voice' to explain their inspiration and artistic choices. These talking artworks are then compiled into a digital class gallery using Book Creator, celebrating diverse forms of expression and imagination.

Learners document their creative process by capturing photos and videos of their artwork, poetry, and music explorations in Seesaw. They use drawing and voice annotation tools to reflect on how their imagination and appreciation of others' work has grown, building a rich, multimedia portfolio of their artistic journey.

Students use Canva's highly visual templates to design vibrant posters that showcase different forms of artistic expression (art, poetry, music, dance). They combine their own photos, drawings, and Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to highlight how creativity and imagination can be communicated in unique ways.

Using Puppet Pals on iPads, students create and voice original short plays or fairy tales, experimenting with character, emotion, and perspective. This hands-on digital puppetry encourages risk-taking and empathy as students bring their imaginative stories to life and share them with the class.

Students use the Design Cycle to plan, prototype, and build a large floor maze inspired by famous artworks or their own creative ideas. They program Bee-Bots to navigate the maze, explaining how their design choices reflect artistic appreciation and imagination. The challenge integrates spatial reasoning, coding, and collaborative creativity in a hands-on STEM context.

plannerText: This unit explores how artistic appreciation and expressions inspire imagination and creativity in Year 2 students. Learners engage with diverse forms of expression?including art, poetry, and music?through hands-on activities, collaborative projects, and reflection, building empathy, risk-taking, and appreciation for their own and others' creativity.

ci: Marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities.
lo: Needs and wants; The changing needs of communities; The design process

s.d: Launch a 'Virtual Marketplace Empathy Walk': Students use Delightex to collaboratively design a digital map of a marketplace, placing virtual 'stalls' that represent different needs and wants in their community. Then, using iPads, they record short Seesaw video reflections as they 'visit' each stall, role-playing as community members with different backgrounds (e.g., a new arrival, an elderly resident, a child with accessibility needs). This immersive experience fosters empathy and deepens understanding of how marketplaces reflect diverse needs and wants.

Students photograph objects from home or the classroom that represent a 'need' or 'want', then use ChatterPix Kids to animate the objects and record short persuasive pitches explaining why their item is essential or desirable in a marketplace. These can be shared in a class 'Marketplace Gallery' to spark discussion about value and perspective.

Create a collaborative digital 'Needs & Wants Marketplace Book' where each student authors a page featuring a product they designed to meet a specific community need. Pages include photos, drawings, and voice recordings, making the book highly visual and accessible for Year 2 learners.

Facilitate a 'Market Research Collage' activity: students visit a local market (virtually or in person), take photos using iPad cameras, and use PicCollage to sort and label items as 'needs' or 'wants'. They can add stickers and voice notes to explain their reasoning, then present their findings to the class.

Guide students to create simple animated stories in ScratchJr where characters must make choices between needs and wants in a marketplace scenario. Students program their characters to interact, negotiate, and reflect on the consequences of their decisions, reinforcing the central idea through creative coding.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype and build a 'Mini-Market Maze' for Bee-Bots. Working in teams, they design and construct stalls representing different needs and wants, then program Bee-Bots to navigate the maze to collect essential items for various community members. This hands-on project integrates design thinking, spatial reasoning, and collaboration.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 2 students explore how marketplaces reflect the needs and wants of communities, with a strong focus on distinguishing between needs and wants, understanding changing community needs, and using the design process to create solutions. Activities include role-play, sorting games, design thinking, and a culminating project where students design products to meet real community needs.

ci: Forces and motion are all around us.
lo: Forces around us; Scientific laws of motion; Innovations in technology

s.d: Transport students into a fully immersive, interactive world of forces and motion using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes. Students can manipulate digital objects, observe how different forces act on them, and even 'walk around' simple machines to see push, pull, gravity, and friction in action from every angle. This hands-on, spatial experience will make abstract concepts tangible and unforgettable.

s.t: App Smashing: iPad Camera + ChatterPix Kids + Seesaw
s.d: Students take photos around the school playground of real-life examples of forces (e.g., swings, slides, ramps) using the iPad Camera. They then use ChatterPix Kids to animate these objects, having them 'talk' about the forces acting on them, before uploading their creations to Seesaw for class sharing and reflection.

Students design and code simple animations or games that demonstrate push, pull, gravity, and friction. For example, they can create a scene where a character moves differently on ice versus grass, or where a ball is pushed up and down a ramp, visually exploring the effects of different forces.

Learners create interactive digital books documenting their experiments with forces and motion. Each page can feature photos, voice recordings, and drawings showing their predictions, observations, and conclusions from hands-on activities like car ramps, magnet mazes, and friction tests.

Students create vibrant, visual collages comparing different types of forces and simple machines. They can use photos from their playground walk, stickers, and labels to sort and classify examples of push, pull, friction, and gravity, then present their collages to the class.

Using the Design Cycle, students work in teams to design and build obstacle courses for Bee-Bots that require navigating ramps, bumps, and turns. They iterate on their designs to explore how different surfaces and inclines affect the Bee-Bot's movement, documenting their process with photos and reflections in Seesaw.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in hands-on investigations of forces and motion, including push, pull, gravity, friction, and simple machines. Students explore these concepts through playground observations, experiments with ramps and magnets, creative projects like Rube Goldberg machines, and reflective activities that connect scientific thinking to real-world play and innovation.

ci: Taking care of our Earth starts with the choices we make.
lo: Earth's resources; How our actions affect the environment; Ways we can care for our planet

s.d: Launch an immersive 'Earth Guardians' VR experience where students use ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes to virtually explore Earth's resources, pollution hotspots, and endangered habitats. Students can manipulate 3D models of ecosystems, observe the impact of human choices in real time, and record their reflections as short Seesaw videos, making abstract environmental concepts tangible and memorable.

App smash ChatterPix Kids and PicCollage: Students photograph items from their lunchboxes or classroom, use ChatterPix to make the items 'speak' about their environmental impact, and then create a visual collage advocating for better choices. This empowers students to become persuasive advocates for Earth-friendly habits in a playful, creative way.

Students create interactive digital books documenting their week-long 'Waste Detective' investigation, including photos of lunchbox waste, graphs of recyclable vs. landfill items, and voice recordings reflecting on how their choices affect the environment. These books can be shared with families to inspire action beyond the classroom.

Facilitate a 'Global Earth Care Challenge' where students document daily actions that help the planet (e.g., turning off lights, composting, picking up litter) with photos and short audio reflections in Seesaw. Connect with a partner class at another Wesley campus to compare and celebrate sustainable choices.

Students embark on the 'Ocean Heroes' Minecraft Education adventure (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/ocean-heroes), rescuing coral reefs and learning real-world conservation skills. This hands-on digital journey directly links to the central idea by showing how individual and collective choices protect Earth's resources.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype and code a 'Clean-Up Crew' Bee-Bot challenge. They design obstacle courses representing polluted environments and program Bee-Bots to collect 'rubbish' (tokens), iterating their designs for maximum efficiency. This tactile, collaborative project reinforces environmental stewardship and computational thinking.

plannerText: The Year 2 'Sharing the Planet' unit centers on the idea that caring for Earth begins with our choices. Students investigate Earth's resources, analyze how their actions impact the environment, and explore practical ways to care for the planet through hands-on inquiry, waste audits, persuasive projects, and community action.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history.
lo: The significance of places to different people and cultures; The connection between landmarks and identity; How landmarks and symbols represent our history

s.d: Students embark on a virtual time-travel adventure using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes to explore iconic Australian and global landmarks in 3D. They can 'hold' and rotate digital models of Uluru, the Sydney Opera House, or the Great Wall of China, then record short ChatterPix Kids voiceovers as if they are the landmark telling its own story, blending immersive tech with creative storytelling.

Learners go on a campus or local walking tour to photograph significant places and symbols using the iPad Camera app. They then upload their images to Seesaw, adding voice recordings or simple labels to explain the significance of each place, fostering connections between personal, local, and cultural landmarks.

Students create a collaborative digital class book in Book Creator, with each child designing a page about a landmark or place of significance (local or global). Pages can include photos, drawings, voice recordings, and simple maps, allowing for multimodal expression and easy sharing with families.

Using Bee-Bots and custom-made floor maps, students program their robots to 'visit' different landmarks placed on a classroom map of Australia or the world. Each stop includes a quick fact or story, reinforcing mapping skills, sequencing, and the connection between place and story.

Students design vibrant digital posters in Canva, showcasing a chosen landmark's story, cultural significance, and a key symbol. They use Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to add themselves into the scene, making the learning personal and visually engaging.

In a hands-on STEM project, students use the Design Cycle to create a 'Landmark Adventure Track' for Sphero Indi cars. They design and build a course representing a journey between significant places (e.g., from their school to a local monument), using colored tiles to code Indi's path. Students iterate on their designs, reflect on challenges, and present their tracks, connecting physical space, storytelling, and computational thinking.

plannerText: This unit immerses Year 2 students in exploring how landmarks and places of significance tell stories about our history. Through mapping, storytelling, Indigenous perspectives, and hands-on inquiry, learners connect with local and national sites, investigate the meaning behind symbols and monuments, and create multimodal representations to deepen their understanding of place, culture, and memory.

ci: Our relationships with others impact our lives.
lo: Importance of relationships; Developing and maintaining healthy relationships; The impact of relationships on wellbeing

s.d: Launch a 'Relationship Simulator' using Delightex where students visually map out different types of relationships (friends, family, classmates) and use branching scenarios to explore how choices and actions impact feelings and outcomes. Students then reflect by recording voice notes or drawing their own relationship stories in Seesaw, app-smashing both platforms for a dynamic, interactive empathy-building experience.

Students take photos of themselves or classroom objects and use ChatterPix Kids to animate them, giving voice to different perspectives in a friendship scenario. For example, they can make a lunchbox 'talk' about how it feels when shared or not shared, directly linking to the central idea of relationships impacting lives.

Create a collaborative digital class book in Book Creator where each student adds a page about a time when a relationship (friend, family, teacher, pet) made a difference in their life. Students can use photos, drawings, and voice recordings to make it highly visual and personal.

Students design and act out short digital puppet shows in Puppet Pals, dramatizing real-life playground or classroom scenarios that require empathy, problem-solving, or conflict resolution. This helps them practice and reflect on healthy relationship skills in a creative, low-pressure way.

Have students create a visual 'Friendship Recipe' in PicCollage, combining photos, stickers, and drawings to represent the ingredients of a healthy relationship (e.g., kindness, listening, sharing). They can share their recipes with the class and discuss how each 'ingredient' impacts their daily interactions.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students work in teams to design and map out a 'Friendship Maze' for Bee-Bots. Each section of the maze represents a different relationship challenge (e.g., sharing, apologizing, including others). Students use the Design Cycle to plan, build, test, and improve their maze, then program the Bee-Bot to navigate it, reflecting on how positive actions help overcome obstacles in relationships.

plannerText: This year-long Year 2 unit explores how our relationships with others impact our lives, focusing on the importance of relationships, developing and maintaining healthy connections, and understanding the changeable nature of relationships. Students engage in activities that build social-emotional intelligence, empathy, and cooperative skills, supported by explicit teaching of self-regulation, friendship strategies, and reflective practices.

ci: Celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community.
lo: The ways personal and cultural events are celebrated; Our appreciation of the diversity of cultures; How celebrations shape community identity

s.d: Immerse students in a global celebration scavenger hunt using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes. Students can virtually explore and interact with 3D models of traditional artifacts, foods, and festival scenes from around the world, then record their reflections as digital postcards to share on Seesaw.

Students create interactive digital infographics or posters in Canva that showcase the unique traditions, foods, music, and symbols of their own or a peer's cultural background, integrating images, voice recordings, and embedded videos to bring their research to life.

Facilitate a collaborative digital book in Book Creator where each student authors a page about a celebration or tradition from their family or heritage, including photos, drawings, and audio explanations. The final book can be shared with the school community or families.

Guide students to document their family traditions by interviewing family members using the iPad Camera app, then upload and annotate their videos, photos, and voice notes in Seesaw. Encourage students to comment on each other's posts to foster intercultural understanding.

Challenge students to design a short, animated video or digital story in Adobe Express that explains the significance of a celebration or tradition, using music, narration, and visual effects to engage their audience.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype and code a moving parade float or festival scene with Lego Spike Prime, representing a celebration from their own or another culture. They document their design process and present their working models, explaining how their creation expresses cultural identity and community values.

plannerText: In this unit, students explore how celebrations and traditions shape the culture of a community by investigating personal and global cultural events, appreciating diversity, and reflecting on what it means to be a global citizen. Activities include mind mapping traditions, researching and presenting cultural identities, creating digital presentations, and collaborating on creative projects that highlight the richness of multicultural Australia.

ci: The rights and responsibilities of citizens are determined by the rules and laws of a community.
lo: The differences between rules and laws; People responsible for enforcing rules and laws; Rights and responsibilities of citizens; The consequences when rules, laws and rights are not met

s.d: Launch a 'Virtual Parliament' experience where students use ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes to explore 3D models of government buildings, courtrooms, and community spaces. Students can interact with virtual scenarios to make decisions as citizens, judges, or lawmakers, experiencing firsthand how rules and laws are created and enforced.

Students design persuasive digital posters or infographics advocating for a new school rule or community law, using Canva's templates and visual tools to communicate the importance of rights and responsibilities. These can be shared in a virtual gallery for peer voting and feedback.

Create interactive digital storybooks in Book Creator where students write and illustrate stories about characters navigating rules, laws, and consequences in different communities. Incorporate voice recordings and clickable elements for an engaging, multimodal experience.

Facilitate a collaborative 'Global Laws Wall' on Padlet where students research and post about unusual or interesting laws from around the world, then comment on how these laws reflect different community values and rights. Encourage students to draw comparisons and discuss fairness and responsibility.

Students explore the 'Active Citizen' Minecraft Education world to learn about Nobel Peace Laureates and how laws and rights shape communities. They can build their own community spaces and propose laws, reflecting on the impact of their decisions. https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/active-citizen

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to invent and prototype an interactive 'Citizenship Game Show' using Makey Makey and laptops. They design physical buzzers and code quiz questions about rules, rights, and responsibilities, then host a live game show for peers to reinforce learning through play.

plannerText: This unit engages students in exploring the differences between rules and laws, the people responsible for enforcing them, and the rights and responsibilities of citizens. Activities include creating persuasive texts, researching global laws, and reflecting on the consequences when rules and rights are not met. Students connect learning to local and global contexts, culminating in collaborative and creative projects that deepen their understanding of citizenship.

ci: Humans use their understanding of scientific principles to take action.
lo: The work of scientists; The process of learning; How science brings about new understandings and changes

s.d: Launch students into an immersive 'Scientist's Lab' experience using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes. Students can virtually manipulate molecules, observe states of matter, and simulate energy transfer, then record their discoveries as short video reflections using the iPad camera. This app smash brings abstract scientific principles to life and lets students 'take action' by sharing their findings with the community.

Students create dynamic infographics or animated posters in Canva to showcase the biography and scientific achievements of a chosen scientist, highlighting how their understanding of scientific principles led to real-world action. These can be displayed digitally or printed for a science fair, directly connecting to the unit's focus on scientists' impact.

Have students document their own science experiments and investigations by creating interactive digital science journals in Book Creator. They can include photos, diagrams, voice recordings, and reflections on how they used scientific principles to solve problems or take action, mirroring the scientific method explored in the unit.

Use Seesaw for students to capture and share their science fair project process: from initial questions, hypothesis, experiment setup, to results and reflections. Teachers and peers can provide feedback, fostering a collaborative inquiry community and reinforcing the action-oriented nature of scientific learning.

Students apply their understanding of scientific measurement by designing and solving real-world area and volume challenges in Minecraft Education. This lesson (https://education.minecraft.net/en-us/lessons/area-and-volume-2) lets students experiment with scale and space, linking directly to the unit's focus on scientific principles and their applications.

For the Design Cycle STEM project, students use Lego Spike Prime to design and prototype a device that demonstrates a scientific principle (e.g., a heat transfer model, a simple machine, or a fair test experiment). They follow the full design process: investigate, plan, create, test, and improve, then present their working model and learning journey to the class, embodying the unit's central idea of using scientific understanding to take action.

plannerText: This unit engages Year 3 students in exploring how humans use scientific principles to take action, focusing on the work of scientists, the process of scientific learning, and how science brings about new understandings. Students conduct hands-on experiments, research scientists, create science fair projects, and connect their learning to real-world issues and the UN Sustainable Development Goals.

ci: Adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments.
lo: Adaptations and structural features of living things; Roles of living things in an ecosystem; Actions that benefit or harm ecosystems

s.d: Students create and narrate immersive 360-degree virtual field trips exploring endangered ecosystems, using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes to interact with 3D models of animals and their adaptations. They can record voiceovers explaining how specific adaptations help survival, directly linking to the unit's focus on adaptation and sustainability.

Students collaboratively design digital infographics or animated posters about animal adaptations and ecosystem threats, using Canva's 'Remove Background' feature to create visually striking, information-rich presentations for their documentaries and assembly presentations.

Learners produce interactive digital books documenting their research on animal adaptations, food webs, and human impacts on ecosystems. These books can include embedded videos, audio reflections, and student-drawn diagrams, supporting multimodal learning and sharing with the school community.

In teams, students script, storyboard, and film mini-documentaries about a chosen ecosystem and its endangered species, integrating interviews, voiceovers, and original footage. They use iMovie to edit and publish their work, culminating in a documentary showcase for parents and peers.

Students build a collaborative digital wall to crowdsource and map global conservation actions, sharing real-world examples of sustainability efforts (aligned with UN Goals 14 & 15). They can post photos, videos, and reflections from their own 'action projects' or research findings.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype and program a robotic animal with Lego Spike Prime, engineering both physical and behavioral adaptations (e.g., camouflage, movement, defense mechanisms). They test their models in simulated habitats, iterating based on peer feedback and reflecting on how design choices support survival in changing environments.

plannerText: In this unit, students investigate how adaptation and sustainability ensure the survival of living things in changing environments. They explore animal adaptations, roles in ecosystems, and human impacts, culminating in team-based documentary projects, digital presentations, and action-oriented reflections aligned with UN sustainability goals.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society.
lo: The factors that motivate exploration (Causation); How people reflect on discoveries from the past to inform change (Change); How exploration brings about change and new understandings for people, places and society (Change)

s.d: Launch an immersive 'Explorer's Museum' where students use ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes to interact with 3D models of ships, artifacts, and maps from the First Fleet and other global explorations. Students can virtually 'handle' historical objects, triggering audio stories and facts, then record their own discoveries as digital museum guides.

Students research a famous explorer or First Fleet figure, then create a dynamic infographic in Canva highlighting their motivations, discoveries, and impact. They upload their infographic to Seesaw and record an audio reflection in character, explaining how exploration shaped society from their perspective.

Students explore and build historically accurate homes from different eras and cultures, including those of First Nations Australians and early settlers. They reflect on how exploration and migration influenced architecture and daily life, then present their builds to the class.

Students create interactive digital journals from the perspective of a First Fleet convict, Indigenous Australian, or explorer. They include diary entries, maps, drawings, and voice recordings to document the journey, discoveries, and societal changes brought by exploration.

Using Google Maps, students plot the routes of famous explorers and the First Fleet, pinning key events and discoveries along the way. They then collaborate on a Padlet timeline, adding images, facts, and reflections about how each journey changed society.

s.t: Lego Spike Prime Design Cycle Challenge
s.d: In teams, students use the Design Cycle to prototype a 'Mars Exploration Rover' with Lego Spike Prime. They research the challenges of Mars exploration, design and build their rover, program it to complete a simulated mission, and present their process and discoveries, linking back to how exploration leads to new societal possibilities.

plannerText: This unit engages students in exploring how exploration leads to new discoveries that can shape and change society, with a focus on the First Fleet, motivations for exploration, and the impact on Indigenous Australians and broader society. Students investigate historical and contemporary explorations, reflect on multiple perspectives, and create projects that connect past discoveries to future possibilities.

ci: An inquiry into the nature of the self; beliefs and values; personal, physical, mental, social and spiritual health; human relationships.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

s.d: Launch students into a fully immersive empathy-building experience by having them create and narrate their own 'Resilience Journey' VR scenes. Using ClassVR headsets and Merge Cubes, students can design 3D story environments that represent personal challenges, strategies for overcoming setbacks, and moments of growth. Their peers can then 'walk through' each other's journeys, fostering deep understanding of diverse perspectives and resilience strategies.

Students design interactive digital posters or infographics that showcase their personal strategies for managing change, respecting others, and keeping themselves safe both online and face-to-face. These can be shared with the class and parents, and include embedded videos or voiceovers reflecting on their learning.

Have students collaboratively author a digital 'Friendology Handbook' using Book Creator, where each page features a scenario about friendship fires, growth mindset, or online safety. Students can app smash by importing illustrations from Brushes Redux or photos from the iPad Camera app.

Empower students to create their own resilience and safety-themed Kahoot quizzes based on the resilience strategies, growth mindset scenarios, and Friendology rules explored in the unit. Students can host these quizzes for peers and even families, reinforcing learning through playful competition.

Facilitate ongoing reflection and peer feedback by having students document their personal growth journeys in Seesaw journals. They can upload videos, audio reflections, and digital artifacts from throughout the unit, tagging specific strategies they've used to overcome challenges or support friends.

STEM Design Cycle Project: Challenge students to prototype a 'Feelings Alert System' using Makey Makey and everyday materials. Students design and build a simple device that can signal when someone is feeling uncomfortable or needs support (e.g., a touchpad that plays a supportive message or lights up). They follow the Design Cycle: empathize (identify needs), ideate, prototype, test, and reflect, directly linking to personal safety and emotional awareness.

plannerText: This unit explores how individuals respond to challenges and setbacks, develop resilience, and manage change, with a strong focus on personal safety (both online and face-to-face), friendship skills, and growth mindset. Students engage in reflective journals, scenario-based activities, collaborative projects, and specialist links (e.g., music, PE, art, drama) to build strategies for wellbeing and respectful relationships.

ci: Technology transforms how we create and share ideas.
lo: How technology expands creative possibilities; The evolution of communication tools; The impact of technology on expression

s.d: Transform the classroom into an immersive digital art gallery by having students design 3D models or digital artworks that represent their personal or cultural stories using Tinkercad or Canva, then upload these creations to Merge Cubes and explore them in ClassVR. Students can record audio narrations explaining their creative process and the meaning behind their work, blending storytelling, technology, and spatial computing in a way that redefines how ideas are shared and experienced.

Students create interactive digital posters or infographics that compare the evolution of communication tools (e.g., cave paintings, telegrams, social media), embedding short video interviews with family members about how technology has changed the way they express themselves. Posters can be shared with the school community to spark intergenerational conversations about digital expression.

Learners author a collaborative digital anthology titled 'Voices of Change,' where each student contributes a multimedia page (text, images, audio, and video) exploring how technology has enabled them to express their identity, passions, or opinions. The anthology can be published and shared with younger students as a model of positive digital citizenship and creative expression.

App smash Seesaw with the iPad Camera to create a 'Day in the Life of a Digital Communicator' video journal. Students document and reflect on all the ways they use technology to communicate throughout a single day, then annotate their videos with voiceovers and digital drawings in Seesaw to highlight ethical choices and cybersafety moments.

Students design and conduct a whole-school survey using Microsoft Forms to investigate how different year levels use technology to express themselves. They analyse the data in Excel, create visual representations, and present their findings to the school, sparking a discussion on ethical and creative technology use across age groups.

Design Cycle STEM Project: Students use the Design Cycle to prototype and code a 'Creative Expression Bot' with Lego Spike Prime. The bot could draw, write, or display digital messages, and students must iterate their designs to solve real-world communication challenges (e.g., how to share ideas with someone who speaks a different language or has a disability). They document their process and present their final bots in a digital showcase, reflecting on how technology transforms creative possibilities.

plannerText: In this unit, Year 4 students explore the central idea that technology transforms how we create and share ideas. They investigate the evolution of communication tools, ethical considerations in digital expression, and cybersafety. The unit culminates in students planning and leading technology workshops for younger students, focusing on creative, safe, and responsible use of digital platforms.

ci: Systems connect us.
lo: Human systems; The interconnections that exist within and across systems; The influence of systems on communities

lo: The causes of geological change over time; How extreme environments cause natural disasters; The impact of natural events on communities

ci: People can create or manipulate messages to target specific audiences.

lo: The power of giving back; How our environment is impacted by the products we create; Sustainable business practices

lo: The nature and impact of natural hazards (Causation); How design processes are used to generate and test solutions (Function); The ethical and practical considerations in human responses to natural challenges (Responsibility)

ci: Finding peaceful solutions to conflict can make a difference in the world.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time

ci: Energy may be converted, transformed, and used to support human progress.
lo: Different forms of energy and how they are used; The impact of different energy sources on the planet; Sustainable energy practices

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Significant events shape a place

lo: How our feelings and emotions affect us; How feelings and emotions are interpreted; How our emotions and behaviour affect relationships

ci: The products people use go through a process of change.
lo: Where food comes from; How food can be changed for different purposes; An appreciation of food

ci: The properties of materials influence how objects move and their use.

ci: An inquiry into rights and responsibilities in the struggle to share finite resources with other people and with other living things.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Family histories shape who we are and what we value
lo: What makes a family; The people in your family; Your family history; What is important to your family

ci: People use imagination and materials to express stories, ideas and culture through performance.
lo: Different materials to make puppets; Types and uses of puppets by different cultures; Features and purposes of puppet performances

ci: Communities work together by creating systems to help people live and learn.
lo: The roles people play in a community; Systems and routines that help us stay organised; How communities work together to meet needs

ci: Movement is dependent on different forces.
lo: Ways objects move; The influence different materials have on movement; How designs can be adapted and improved

ci: Invertebrates are essential to living things and the environment.
lo: The characteristics of invertebrates; The interdependence of invertebrates and their environment; Life cycles of invertebrates

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: An understanding of history helps us to see how the past shapes the future.
lo: Ways in which children's lives have changed over time; Reasons for changes to children's lives over time

ci: Emotions, values, and relationships guide how we connect and collaborate
lo: The function of different parts of the brain; Situations that cause pleasant and unpleasant feelings; Actions that support wellbeing

ci: Storytellers use different methods to convey meaning.
lo: Mediums and techniques artists use; Connections between story and illustrations; How ideas and information are communicated

ci: A sequence of processes enables a system to function.
lo: The systems in our lives; The purpose of various systems; How systems are made of many interdependent parts

ci: Changes to materials affect how we use and reuse them in daily life.
lo: Understanding material properties; Reuse and recycling through physical change; Sustainably managing materials

ci: Patterns in the sky help us understand our place in the universe.
lo: Earth's place in the solar system; Observable patterns in the sky; Tools and methods we use to observe the sky

ci: Water is a limited resource that is essential to life.
lo: Unequal access to water; Communities affected by water access; Transferral of water from source to use; Responsibility as global citizens

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploring the past helps us to understand the present.
lo: Evidence and its significance in a place; Connections between the past and present within a place; Personal history shapes the present

ci: Humans have a responsibility in their relationships to value the rights of others
lo: The difference between needs and wants; Everybody has rights and responsibilities; What is a respectful relationship

ci: Creativity allows people to express their understanding of the world.
lo: The ways people use technology to express creativity; How texts and images work together; How technology influences our creativity

ci: Needs can be met with digital systems.
lo: What are digital systems; Ideas and information can be organised and shared in safe online environments; Being a responsible digital citizen

ci: Observing how materials change helps us make sense of the physical world.
lo: Observable properties of solids and liquids; How heating and cooling change state; Scientific thinking helps solve everyday problems

ci: The diversity of soils, rocks, and minerals shapes the way people value and use Earth's resources.
lo: Observable properties of soils, rocks and minerals; Earth's resources are vital and must be used responsibly; First Nations knowledge of soils and rocks

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Land and place are represented and valued in different ways.
lo: How Australia and neighbouring regions define places; Australia's states/territories have unique identities; First Nations connections to Country

ci: The choices and actions we make contribute to our health and wellbeing.
lo: What it means to be healthy; Choices and their impact on health; Actions to maintain a healthy mind and body

ci: Responding to different artistic expressions can promote thinking.
lo: Ways forms of expression convey messages; Creative and critical thinking skills; The language used to respond to The Arts

ci: Communities create rules, laws and services to help people live together fairly and safely.
lo: Effective and influential leadership; Strengths that help leaders guide others; How leadership impacts communities

ci: The design and use of products can have an impact on the natural world.
lo: How material properties determine their use; Material solutions for a sustainable future; How research solves problems in the natural world

lo: Spiritual or religious beliefs; Similarities and differences of spiritual beliefs; Contributions of different religions to human diversity

ci: People design for purpose.
lo: How design thinking creates in various ways; Design thinking meets community needs; Effective ways to engage an audience

ci: Economic systems are an integral part of human society.
lo: Wealth is more than money; Responsibilities when managing income; Income is earned and managed in many ways

ci: The interactions between living things, materials, and light enable us to make decisions about the world
lo: How structural features and adaptations help living things survive; How weathering and erosion cause change to Earth's surface; How light is used in science and technology

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways.
lo: The need for conservation; Community values inform management of the natural environment; Ways environmental stewardship can drive change

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: The discovery of resources has impacted the growth and development of nations.
lo: Significance of resource discovery in development of societies; Impact of migration on cultural, social and economic growth; Exploration and settlement effects on Indigenous and colonial populations

ci: Managing change requires acceptance, knowledge and strategies.
lo: Establishing, managing and maintaining relationships; Managing behaviours in digital situations; Managing changes associated with puberty

ci: The media engages its audience and invites a response
lo: How media targets audiences; Techniques used to engage and persuade; Critical evaluation of media messages

ci: An inquiry into the interconnectedness of human-made systems and communities; the structure and function of organisations.
lo: Lines of inquiry to be confirmed once planner is uploaded

ci: Scientists undertake experimentation to confirm, contest and create new theories.
lo: The scientific method; How experiments lead to new understandings; The relationship between planets and the Sun; Energy transfer and transformation in circuits

ci: The influences on our consumer choices reveal the interdependence between human and natural systems and call for responsible action toward just and sustainable futures.
lo: Influences on consumer choices (function); How consumer decisions impact living things and their habitats (causation); Alternative models of consumerism that honour a fair and sustainable future (perspective)

ci: Human actions change the environment.
lo: How people interact with different environments; The effect people have on the environment; Ways in which people can take action and care for the environment

lo: How our brain is growing and changing; How our emotions, behaviours and actions promote relationships; How we care for our bodies

ci: Systems are involved in preparing products for consumers.
lo: Where products come from; The systems products go through; How people choose their products

ci: Weather affects our lives in different ways.

ci: Living things have structures that allow them to survive in different environments.

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: History and culture connect the past, present and future.

ci: We learn about ourselves through the games we play.
lo: How to identify and manage emotions; How players' choices affect relationships; Our responsibilities when playing games

ci: Artefacts provide a window into the beliefs and values of cultures.
lo: Beliefs and values contribute to a culture; The importance of rituals and traditions; How artefacts capture history and cultural expression

ci: Technology can influence the way ecosystems can survive.
lo: An ecosystem has animals, plants and microorganisms; Technology can cause ecosystems to adapt and evolve; Technology can help or hinder ecosystems

ci: People and the planet are reliant on different energy sources.
lo: Energy types (electricity, fossil fuels, solar); Energy helps us in daily life; Energy transformation; Responsibility to conserve energy and find sustainable sources

ci: To sustain life, people and environments depend on one another.
lo: Interdependence of people and environments; How people's wants and needs affect the land; Responsibility to use sustainable resources; Connection between fossil fuels and environmental impact

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Journeys can lead to challenges and new understandings.
lo: Types of journeys (physical, learning, cultural); Challenges encountered on journeys; New understandings gained through journeys

ci: The way we interact with others is influenced by who we are
lo: Personal identity and strengths; How self-regulation supports learning; Building resilience through knowledge of ourselves

ci: Methods of communication have different features which impact their effectiveness
lo: The forms of verbal, non-verbal and visual communication; How the correct form enhances the effectiveness of a message; How people interpret messages and bring their own meaning to interactions

ci: Ethical decision making supports online choices.
lo: How choice of action and reaction affects others online; How informed choices can make a positive difference; Our right to feel safe online and the responsibility to make ethical decisions

ci: Materials behave and interact in certain ways, which poses challenges and solutions
lo: The three states of matter and their properties; How matter changes states depending on particles, temperature and energy; Responsible and ethical use of materials like glass and plastic

ci: People can respond to natural disasters with resilience
lo: The causes and types of natural disasters (tectonic events, weather events); How natural disasters impact the environment and people; How humans respond with resilience and connection

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Many factors shape the development of cities.
lo: The essential features of a city; The function of a city; The development of cities through time

ci: Health and wellbeing are impacted by the choices we make
lo: How our body systems connect and function; How our choices affect physical, emotional and mental wellbeing; Strategies to measure and maintain the health of the human body

ci: The creative process involves understanding and applying artistic elements to communicate ideas and emotions across disciplines
lo: How colour, movement and music communicate character and mood; The creative process of making a multi-disciplinary presentation; The effort and commitment required to produce a live performance

ci: Not-for-profit organisations exist to respond to a variety of needs within our community
lo: The function and purpose of not-for-profit organisations; How not-for-profit organisations respond to community needs; Our responsibility to take action and make a difference

ci: Engineers design and build structures using specific features and materials to make them strong, stable and fit for purpose
lo: Different types of bridges and their design features; How materials and engineering principles contribute to structural strength and stability; Testing and refining designs through experimentation

ci: First Nations people have a deep connection to Country, language and culture
lo: The diversity of language and culture across First Nations communities in Australia; The significance of connection to Country as custodial responsibility; Listening to and learning from Indigenous perspectives

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Exploration and discovery have led to changes that shape the world we live in
lo: The age of exploration and the reasons explorers set out on their journeys; How Australia changed through European arrival; Different perspectives on the consequences of exploration

ci: Leaders can help a group to function effectively
lo: Roles and responsibilities of effective leadership; Purpose of leadership; The strengths and qualities of a leader

ci: Social norms and stereotypes can influence the way people express themselves
lo: How stereotypes and social norms form and persist in society; The impact of discrimination, prejudice and assumptions on individuals and communities; How people overcome stereotypes to express themselves and inspire change

ci: Goods and services are exchanged within a marketplace
lo: The production of goods from raw materials through manufacturing to consumer; The exchange of currency for goods and services in a functioning economy; Ethics and responsible decision-making in the marketplace

ci: Inventions and initiatives are a response to the changing needs of society
lo: How inventions have changed over time to meet the demands of society; The UN Sustainable Development Goals and the global problems they address; Using the design thinking process to create innovations that respond to real-world needs

ci: Communities interact with the environment in different ways
lo: How community values shape the management of the natural environment; The need for conservation and environmental stewardship; Ways in which First Nations land management practices can drive positive environmental change

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Colonisation has had a lasting impact on different countries and their people
lo: The reasons, methods and impact of colonisation; How colonisation transformed society and culture; The responsibility of colonisers and the perspectives of those colonised

ci: Values, beliefs and actions contribute to a diverse society
lo: How our values and beliefs shape our identity and interactions; Our responsibility to manage emotions and be safe online and in person; The importance of resilience, respect and positive relationships in a diverse community

ci: Understanding global issues and using effective communication empowers us to raise awareness and inspire action.
lo: Global issues are caused by interconnected factors that require collective solutions (Causation) ??? Presentations in the media can reflect the perspective of the author rather than the truth (Perspective) ??? We must make responsible choices when responding to and creating media (Responsibility) ??? Messages can be expressed through a range of mediums and elements can be adjusted to enhance effectiveness (Communication) ??? Design choices contribute to the effectiveness of a message (Influence)

ci: The way a country is governed impacts the lives of citizens.
lo: The key institutions and functions of Australia's democratic system of government (Function) ??? How government decisions impact citizens at federal, state and local levels (Causation) ??? The rights and responsibilities of Australian citizens and their obligations as global citizens (Equality/Citizenship)

ci: The relationships between the Earth, Moon and Sun create predictable patterns that shape life on our planet and are understood through both scientific and cultural perspectives.
lo: The movement of the Earth, Moon and Sun causes predictable patterns such as day/night, moon phases, tides and seasons (Causation) ??? Different cultures, including Indigenous knowledge systems, interpret the night sky in unique and meaningful ways (Perspective) ??? The Earth, Moon and Sun have specific structures, positions and relationships that can be represented through models and simulations (Form)

ci: Peoples' actions affect the balance of nature.
lo: What the environment is comprised of and how ecosystems function (Form) ??? Our responsibility to care for and protect the natural world (Responsibility) ??? The interdependence between humans, animals and the environment (Interdependence) ??? The consequences of human actions on the balance of nature (Consequences)

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
ci: Human migration is a response to challenges, risks and opportunities.
lo: The causes for and consequences of migration (Causation) ??? The changes in societies as a result of migration (Change) ??? The impact of migration on the individual, both positive and negative (Impact) ??? Migration results in a more diverse society (Diversity)

ci: Understanding ourselves, our relationships and our wellbeing empowers us to thrive.
lo: The recognisable features of safe and healthy interactions (Form) ??? The connections between emotions, behaviour, learning, interactions and goals (Connection) ??? The tools and skills to stay safe online and in person (Safety) ??? The active role we play in achieving and maintaining our wellbeing (Wellbeing) ??? The strategies to achieve and maintain positive relationships (Relationships)

th: Where We Are in Place and Time
